---
title: "Exhibits"
---

::: {.hero}
<span class="eyebrow">The collection</span>

## Objects that redesigned everyday life
:::

## Permanent collection

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
<span class="index-number">01</span>

### Typewriters to Trackpads
How human-computer interaction evolved from mechanical keys to gesture and
voice. Highlights include an 1874 Sholes & Glidden, a Xerox Alto, and the
first commercial trackpad.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
<span class="index-number">02</span>

### The Chair as Machine
Seating as a design problem: bentwood, tubular steel, injection-molded
plastic, and the ergonomic office chair. Objects from Thonet, Breuer, Eames,
and Herman Miller.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
<span class="index-number">03</span>

### Circuits & Silicon
Vacuum tubes, transistors, and the first integrated circuits, traced through
to the microprocessors that put a computer on every desk.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
<span class="index-number">04</span>

### Household Automation
Toasters, washing machines, and thermostats: the quiet redesign of domestic
life, and what it reveals about who that redesign was for.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
<span class="index-number">05</span>

### Interfaces You Can Touch
A hands-on gallery of dials, switches, sliders, and touchscreens spanning
seventy years of trying to make machines easier to operate.
:::

:::

::: {.banner}
<span class="eyebrow">Rotating exhibition · on view through the end of the season</span>

### "Prototypes That Almost Were"
A look at design dead ends: the products that shaped what came after them
without ever reaching a shop shelf. For comparison, see MoMA's own
[*Prototypes That Almost Were*](https://www.moma.org/calendar/exhibitions/5221)
exhibition.
:::
